---
title: "Unit 4 — Bayesian Inference, Exact & Conjugate"
subtitle: "Predictive variance · the exact coin-game posterior (Beta–Binomial) · Inverse-Gamma conjugacy"
engine: markdown
---

## Overview

This unit is exact Bayesian inference — closed-form posteriors, no approximation yet. You'll
put **error bars** on a regression model via its predictive variance, compute the **exact
posterior** of a coin's bias from a Beta prior and observed tosses (Beta–Binomial
**conjugacy**), and derive the **Inverse-Gamma** conjugate posterior for a regression's noise
variance — which closes the loop back to Unit 1's model, where the noise was treated as known.
The thread throughout: what conjugacy buys you, and where it stops.

## Learning objectives

By the end of Unit 4 you will be able to:

- **Compute the parameter covariance and predictive variance** (error bars) for a polynomial
  fit. *(Apply)*
- **Derive and implement the exact Beta–Binomial posterior** and its marginal likelihood.
  *(Apply)*
- **Derive the Inverse-Gamma conjugate posterior** for the noise variance. *(Analyze)*
- **Interpret** error bars, prior sensitivity, and what conjugacy buys. *(Evaluate)*

## What you'll do

Three parts:

- **(A) Predictive variance** *(code)* — on a synthetic regression dataset with a deliberate
  data gap, compute the parameter covariance
  $\mathrm{cov}\{\widehat{\mathbf{w}}\}=\widehat\sigma^2(\mathbf X^\top\mathbf X)^{-1}$ and the
  predictive variance, and plot **error bars** that widen in the gap and at the edges.
- **(B) The coin-game posterior** *(code)* — with a Beta$(a,b)$ prior on the bias and $y_N$
  heads in $N$ tosses, compute the exact conjugate posterior
  $\text{Beta}(a+y_N,\,b+N-y_N)$, its marginal likelihood, and the probability of winning.
- **(C) Inverse-Gamma conjugacy** *(derivation)* — derive the posterior parameters for the
  noise variance.

Then answer three interpretation questions. **Any correct method passes** — the checks are
properties and relationships, not brittle array values.

## Prerequisites & connections

**Review first.** The matrix linear model and design matrix from Unit 1; the Beta distribution
and its moments from Unit 2b; basic Bayesian vocabulary (prior, likelihood, posterior).

**Connections.**

> The Beta pseudo-counts here are the **Unit 2b Beta moments in action**, and the
> Inverse-Gamma part puts a prior on the **noise variance of Unit 1's Olympic regression**
> (which had treated $\sigma^2$ as known). Both are **conjugate updates** — prior family in,
> same family out — and these exact posteriors become the targets that **Unit 5 approximates**
> when conjugacy runs out.

## How it's graded

Three bundles, specifications not points. **Correctness** (core/gateway) mixes **autograded**
code checks (predictive variance and the coin posterior — property and relationship tests, no
brittle pins) with one **human-graded derivation** (the Inverse-Gamma posterior).
**Interpretation** scores your CERL answers (Claim, Evidence, Reasoning, Limits) and
**Process** covers a cheap import and a clean render. Any unmet bundle gets one
**revise-and-resubmit**; autograded resubmissions are unlimited until the deadline. Full policy
in [`GRADING.md`](../GRADING.md).

## What to submit

- Your completed **`hw.py`** — `PolynomialRegressionModel`, `generate_synthetic_data`, `B`,
  `Beta`, `posterior`, `calculate_marginal_likelihood`, `calculate_probability_of_winning`
  (signatures unchanged).
- Your **derivation write-up** — the Inverse-Gamma conjugate posterior (D1).
- Your **rendered notebook** with the error-bar and posterior figures and interpretation
  answers (I1–I3).

*Submit via the course page — [submit mechanism TBD].*

## Deadline & revision window

- Due: **[PLACEHOLDER]**
- Post-feedback revision window: **[PLACEHOLDER]**

## Estimated effort

Roughly **5–7 hours [PROPOSED]** — two coded parts plus one derivation; the predictive-variance
bookkeeping and the conjugacy reasoning take the most time.
